rationale_for_baseline_tier: Uncomplicated head lice is benign and self-managed with a pediculicide, wet-combing and household measures (T0). Secondary bacterial infection from scratching routes to GP/urgent care (T2/T4).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The scratched scalp/neck becomes swollen, hot, bright red or leaks yellow pus/honey-crusting, or the child develops a fever or very swollen tender neck glands
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched scalp or neck becomes swollen, hot, bright red or leaks yellow pus, or she gets a fever — see a doctor / urgent care.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Shared bedroom/siblings — high household/school transmission risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise treating affected contacts and decontamination to prevent re-infestation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Use a proper lice treatment now and again in 7 days, wet-comb with conditioner every couple of days for about 10 days, check/treat affected family members, and wash bedding/clothes in hot water. The itch settles over a few days.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with pediculicide + repeat; GP/urgent care for secondary infection
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: GP/urgent care if the scalp becomes infected (crusting/pus/fever).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Pediculosis capitis (live lice seen); secondary infection screened negative; pediculicide + day-7 repeat + wet-combing + household measures; antihistamine dosing deferred to weight-based in-person.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: No secondary-infection safety-net for an excoriated scalp.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An impetiginised/cellulitic scalp is missed and the infection spreads.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Omitting the day-7 repeat / household measures, so the infestation recurs.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for uncomplicated head lice — unnecessary.
